questions.q: In perfect competition, what happens to market price when demand increases while supply remains constant?
options: Price increases until a new equilibrium is reached
Price remains unchanged due to perfect competition
Price decreases due to increased consumer spending
Price fluctuates randomly
questions.explanation: When demand increases with constant supply, the quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied, creating a shortage. This pressure causes price to rise until equilibrium is restored. The misconception that perfect competition prevents price changes is incorrect—perfect competition means many firms and price-taking behavior, not rigid prices.

questions.q: A good has price elasticity of demand of -0.5. If price increases by 10%, what is the approximate change in quantity demanded?
options: Decreases by 5%
Decreases by 10%
Increases by 5%
Increases by 0.5%
questions.explanation: Elasticity measures the percentage change in quantity demanded per percentage change in price. With elasticity of -0.5, a 10% price increase causes quantity demanded to fall by approximately 5% (0.5 × 10%). This indicates inelastic demand where quantity responds less than proportionally to price changes.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes marginal utility?
options: The additional satisfaction from consuming one more unit of a good
The total satisfaction from consuming all units of a good
The lowest utility a consumer can experience from a good
The difference between price and utility of a good
questions.explanation: Marginal utility is the incremental satisfaction gained from consuming one additional unit. Total utility (option B) sums all satisfaction, not the marginal addition. Marginal utility typically diminishes as consumption increases, which explains why consumers demand less at higher prices.

questions.q: Under monopolistic competition, what is the primary characteristic that distinguishes it from perfect competition?
options: Product differentiation allows firms to have some price-setting power
There are barriers to entry that prevent new competitors
Firms earn permanent economic profits in long-run equilibrium
Only one dominant firm controls the entire market
questions.explanation: Monopolistic competition features product differentiation (brand, quality, design differences), giving each firm some ability to set prices above marginal cost. Perfect competition assumes homogeneous products. While monopolistically competitive markets have free entry (option B is wrong), firms earn zero economic profits long-run, not permanent profits.

questions.q: A farmer produces wheat. When the market price of wheat rises, the farmer increases output. This illustrates the principle of:
options: Positive slope of the supply curve
Law of diminishing marginal returns
Inelastic demand for agricultural products
Economies of scale in farming
questions.explanation: The supply curve slopes upward because producers increase quantity supplied when price rises, making it more profitable to produce more. This is the fundamental law of supply. Diminishing marginal returns (option B) explains why costs eventually rise with output, but doesn't directly cause the supply curve's positive slope.

questions.q: If a consumer's income increases and they demand less of a normal good, what type of good must it be?
options: An inferior good
A complementary good
A substitute good
A luxury good
questions.explanation: An inferior good is one for which demand decreases when income increases. Examples include generic brands or used clothing. Normal goods show opposite behavior—demand increases with income. The consumer is replacing the lower-quality inferior good with superior alternatives now affordable at higher income levels.

questions.q: At the profit-maximizing point for a perfectly competitive firm, what is the relationship between price, marginal cost, and average total cost?
options: Price equals marginal cost, but may be above or below average total cost
Price equals both marginal cost and average total cost
Price is always below both marginal cost and average total cost
Price exceeds average total cost by the amount of economic profit
questions.explanation: Profit maximization occurs where P = MC for competitive firms (they're price-takers). However, the firm can still earn profit if P > ATC, break even if P = ATC, or suffer loss if P < ATC. Option B incorrectly assumes P always equals ATC—this only occurs in long-run equilibrium, not at each firm's optimal quantity.

questions.q: How does a binding price ceiling affect a competitive market in equilibrium?
options: Creates a shortage as quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied
Creates a surplus as quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded
Causes no effect on market equilibrium if it's binding
Increases both price and quantity in the long run
questions.explanation: A binding price ceiling set below equilibrium price forces transactions below the market-clearing level. Consumers want to buy more at the low price, but producers supply less, creating excess demand (shortage). Non-binding ceilings don't affect equilibrium. This shortage can lead to queuing, rationing, or black markets.

questions.q: Two goods are complements if an increase in the price of one good leads to:
options: A decrease in the demand for the other good
An increase in the demand for the other good
No change in the quantity demanded of the other good
A decrease in the supply of the other good
questions.explanation: Complementary goods are consumed together (like coffee and sugar). When coffee becomes more expensive, fewer people buy coffee, so demand for sugar falls too. Substitute goods show opposite behavior—higher price of one increases demand for the other. Supply shifts (option D) are independent of complementarity.

questions.q: When a monopoly charges different prices to different customers for the same product, this practice is called:
options: Price discrimination
Predatory pricing
Peak load pricing
Price skimming
questions.explanation: Price discrimination occurs when a monopoly sells identical goods at different prices to separate customer groups based on willingness to pay. Predatory pricing (option B) is pricing below cost to eliminate competitors. Price skimming (option D) involves high initial prices later lowered, not differentiation between simultaneous customers.
